commands.review_summary:
The PR scopes gateway startup metadata manifest reconstruction to a conservative plugin-id set derived from restrictive plugin config and installed-index startup metadata.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows the PR writes and consumes `startup.configPaths`, while the persisted installed-index Zod schema still omits that field, so a persisted index read can drop the metadata needed for scoped startup.
commands.review_followup: There is a narrow, source-backed repair: preserve the new index field through persisted schema parsing and add focused roundtrip coverage.

target.title: [AI-assisted] fix(plugins): scope startup metadata manifest reads
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Gateway startup could still rebuild plugin metadata from every installed plugin manifest before narrowing to the configured startup set.
- Solution: carry a conservative startup plugin-id scope into metadata snapshot loading when the Installed Plugin Index can prove the narrowed set safely.
- What changed: metadata snapshots can carry scoped `pluginIds`; lookup/startup paths pass a conservative `pluginIdScope`; startup scoping carries indexed `activation.onConfigPaths` owners such as `browser`; scoped snapshots are not reused for configless default discovery.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no plugin config shape changes, no provider/channel activation semantics changes, no compat bundled-discovery shortcut.

## Motivation

#70533 is a startup stability problem: a restrictive plugin setup should not crash-loop or pay full manifest reconstruction cost just because unrelated installed plugins exist. This keeps the startup path on the Installed Plugin Index when it can safely prove the manifest subset, and falls back to the old unscoped behavior when it cannot.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #70533
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: restrictive gateway startup plugin scope still caused full installed-index manifest reconstruction before startup narrowing.
- Real environment tested: isolated Linux testserver disposable repo, Node v22.22.2, pnpm 11.1.2, `origin/main` at `a13468320c` compared with this patch.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran TSX diagnostic scripts through the real `loadPluginLookUpTable -> loadPluginMetadataSnapshot -> loadPluginManifestRegistryForInstalledIndex -> resolveGatewayStartupPluginPlanFromRegistry` path using valid 96-plugin Installed Plugin Index fixtures.
- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "label": "after-rebased-patch-confirm",
  "indexPluginCount": 96,
  "manifestPluginCount": 1,
  "startupPluginIds": ["openai"],
  "loadedPluginIds": ["openai"],
  "diagnosticCount": 0
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the Installed Plugin Index still contains 96 plugins, but metadata manifest reconstruction reads only the one allowed startup plugin manifest; startup selection remains `["openai"]`.
- Additional blocker-fix proof:

```json
{
  "config": {
    "browser": { "enabled": true },
    "channels": {},
    "plugins": {
      "allow": ["openai"],
      "slots": { "memory": "none" }
    }
  },
  "results": [
    {
      "label": "indexed-config-path-scope",
      "indexPluginCount": 96,
      "manifestPluginCount": 2,
      "startupPluginIds": ["openai", "browser"],
      "loadedPluginIds": ["browser", "openai"],
      "diagnosticCount": 0
    },
    {
      "label": "legacy-index-safe-fallback",
      "indexPluginCount": 96,
      "manifestPluginCount": 96,
      "startupPluginIds": ["openai", "browser"],
      "diagnosticCount": 0
    }
  ]
}
```

- Observed blocker-fix result: `browser.enabled=true` is preserved under a restrictive `plugins.allow=["openai"]`; new indexes stay scoped to 2 manifests, while legacy indexes without indexed config-path metadata fall back unscoped instead of losing `browser`.
- What was not tested: no production credentials, no persistent services, no real external provider/channel account.
- Before evidence:

```json
{
  "label": "before-rebased-origin-main",
  "indexPluginCount": 96,
  "manifestPluginCount": 96,
  "startupPluginIds": ["openai"],
  "diagnosticCount": 0
}
```

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: metadata snapshot loading had no way to receive a conservative startup plugin-id scope after the Installed Plugin Index was available, so it reconstructed the installed manifest registry for the full index before startup planning narrowed plugin ids.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered startup narrowing and metadata memoization separately, but not the manifest reconstruction count for restrictive gateway startup allowlists.
- Contributing context (if known): some startup intent, config-path activation, channel, provider, slot, and harness decisions can only be trusted when the Installed Plugin Index can map them without reading manifests; unsafe cases need to stay unscoped.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/commands/status.summary.redaction.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/plugin-lookup-table.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/channel-plugin-ids.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/plugin-metadata-snapshot.memo.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/current-plugin-metadata-snapshot.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: restrictive `plugins.allow` startup config passes a narrow scope to installed-index manifest reconstruction; config-path activation owners such as `browser` stay included; conservative fallback remains unscoped for compat discovery, legacy indexes, or unmappable providers.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it asserts the actual manifest-registry load boundary without needing external plugin credentials.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none before this PR.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Gateway startup with a restrictive plugin allowlist can avoid reading unrelated installed plugin manifests when the startup scope is safe to prove from the Installed Plugin Index. Unsafe configs keep existing unscoped behavior.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Installed Index (96 plugins) -> read 96 manifests -> startup plan -> openai

After:
Installed Index (96 plugins) -> conservative scope ["browser","openai"] when browser config is present -> read 2 manifests -> startup plan -> openai + browser
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux testserver disposable run
- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.2, pnpm 11.1.2
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): synthetic installed plugin fixture; no external channel account
- Relevant config (redacted): `plugins.allow=["openai"]`, `plugins.slots.memory="none"`; blocker regression also uses `browser.enabled=true`

### Steps

1. On `origin/main`, run the diagnostic fixture through `loadPluginLookUpTable`.
2. Apply this patch.
3. Run the same diagnostic fixture again.
4. Run focused regression tests, format, typecheck, and targeted lint.

### Expected

- Startup still selects `openai`.
- `browser.enabled=true` still selects `browser` even when `plugins.allow` only lists `openai`.
- Manifest reconstruction only reads the scoped startup plugin(s) after the patch.
- Conservative fallback paths remain unscoped when the Installed Plugin Index cannot prove the mapping.

### Actual

- Before: `manifestPluginCount=96`, `startupPluginIds=["openai"]`.
- After: `manifestPluginCount=1`, `loadedPluginIds=["openai"]`, `startupPluginIds=["openai"]`.
- After blocker repair: `manifestPluginCount=2`, `loadedPluginIds=["browser","openai"]`, `startupPluginIds=["openai","browser"]` for `browser.enabled=true`.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [x] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Validation:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/status.summary.redaction.test.ts src/plugins/plugin-lookup-table.test.ts src/plugins/manifest-registry-installed.test.ts src/plugins/channel-plugin-ids.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-plugins.test.ts src/gateway/server-plugins.test.ts src/plugins/plugin-metadata-snapshot.memo.test.ts src/plugins/current-plugin-metadata-snapshot.test.ts src/plugins/manifest-contract-eligibility.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts
10 files / 269 tests passed

corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/plugins/plugin-metadata-snapshot.ts src/plugins/plugin-metadata-snapshot.types.ts src/plugins/plugin-lookup-table.ts src/plugins/manifest-registry-installed.ts src/plugins/effective-plugin-ids.ts src/plugins/gateway-startup-plugin-ids.ts src/gateway/server-startup-plugins.ts src/gateway/server-plugins.ts src/commands/status.summary.redaction.test.ts
passed

git diff --check
passed

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo
passed

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
passed

node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json <11 changed files>
0 warnings / 0 errors

pnpm check:changed
attempted; conflict/changelog/dependency guards plus core and core-test TypeScript passed, then full core lint was killed by `tsgolint` SIGKILL on the testserver. Targeted Oxlint for the changed repair files passed with 0 warnings / 0 errors.
```

Additional note: `origin/main @ a13468320c` failed `test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json` before this patch because `status.summary.redaction.test.ts` had not been updated for the newer `SessionStatus` fields. This PR includes that test-only fixture update so `check:changed` can pass cleanly on the rebased branch.

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: before/after runtime manifest reconstruction count on the real OpenClaw metadata lookup path; restrictive allowlist scope; config-path activation owner retention; legacy index safe fallback; compat discovery fallback; unmappable provider fallback; scoped/unscoped memo separation; configless scoped snapshot reuse rejection.
- Edge cases checked: disabled plugin entries are removed from the scope, direct configured channels/providers only scope when the Installed Plugin Index can map them, scope ids outside the index and legacy config-path activation metadata fall back to unscoped loading.
- What I did **not** verify: real production provider/channel credentials or long-running gateway service startup.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: overly aggressive startup scoping could hide a plugin whose startup intent is only manifest-visible.
  - Mitigation: scope is only used when allowlists/config entries/config-path activation/slots/direct channel/provider/harness ids can be mapped from the Installed Plugin Index; ambiguous compat, provider, channel, legacy-index, or out-of-index cases return to unscoped manifest reconstruction.

target.title: Strip leaked reasoning preambles before outbound send
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: some providers can leak plain-text reasoning/planning preambles into the final outbound reply even when tagged reasoning stripping already exists.
- Why it matters: users can receive internal-looking assistant text instead of only the intended visible answer.
- What changed: added a last-mile outbound cleaner in reply normalization, plus conservative preamble scoring that now ignores code regions and treats pure CJK as a weaker signal unless paired with other suspicious cues.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not change provider prompting policy, transport behavior, model selection, or force `<final>`-style tagging.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #60551
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: outbound reply normalization relied on tagged scaffolding removal, but plain-text leaked reasoning preambles could still survive to channel delivery.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no final text sanitizer in `normalizeReplyPayloadDirectives(...)` for plain-text planning/reasoning lead-ins.
- Contributing context (if known): GLM/Kimi-style outputs can mix visible replies with untagged internal-looking preambles, and the first heuristic draft needed follow-up hardening to avoid pure-CJK and code-fence false positives.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-delivery.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - suspicious English planning preamble is stripped before visible reply text
  - mixed CJK + suspicious lead-in can still be stripped when signals agree
  - ordinary multi-paragraph CJK replies are preserved
  - suspicious-looking text inside fenced code blocks does not trigger stripping
  - reply normalization applies the cleaner before final send
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in shared text sanitation plus the reply normalization seam, so unit + seam coverage hits the failure line directly without needing full channel E2E.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): tagged scaffolding stripping already existed, but not the plain-text leaked-preamble cases added here.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Final outbound replies are more aggressively cleaned when a suspicious internal-looking preamble appears before a user-facing answer.
- Legitimate multi-paragraph replies are preserved more carefully after the follow-up heuristic hardening.
- Suspicious-looking content inside fenced code blocks no longer contributes to preamble scoring.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[assistant/model output]
  -> [tag stripping only]
  -> [plain-text leaked preamble may survive]
  -> [user sees internal-looking lead-in]

After:
[assistant/model output]
  -> [tag stripping]
  -> [plain-text leaked-preamble cleaner]
  -> [reply normalization]
  -> [user sees only intended visible reply]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node.js / pnpm workspace checkout
- Model/provider: bug motivated by GLM/Kimi-style reply behavior; patch is provider-agnostic in the outbound sanitizer seam
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram/OpenClaw auto-reply path locally, plus shared outbound normalization path
- Relevant config (redacted): default local OpenClaw workspace config; no config schema changes

### Steps

1. Produce reply text that starts with suspicious planning/reasoning lines and then switches into a user-facing answer.
2. Pass that text through `stripAssistantInternalScaffolding(...)` and `normalizeReplyPayloadDirectives(...)`.
3. Compare behavior for normal multi-paragraph replies, CJK-only replies, and fenced-code-block cases.

### Expected

- Internal-looking preambles are removed when a clear visible answer follows.
- Legitimate replies without enough suspicious signal are preserved.
- Code-fenced suspicious text does not trigger stripping.

### Actual

- Updated implementation matches expected behavior in targeted runtime assertions and added tests.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - imported the changed modules successfully with `pnpm exec tsx`
  - ran explicit runtime assertions covering English leaked-preamble stripping, mixed CJK suspicious stripping, normal CJK preservation, fenced-code preservation, and reply-delivery integration
  - patched the local installed OpenClaw runtime and confirmed gateway health after restart
- Edge cases checked:
  - ordinary multi-paragraph English reply with mild meta phrasing
  - ordinary multi-paragraph CJK reply
  - suspicious-looking content inside fenced code blocks
  - reply text reduced to trimmed visible output before send
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full repo-wide `pnpm check` completion in this environment; local pre-commit hook stalled/SIGKILLed inside the broader `tsgo` step, so this PR relies on targeted verification for the touched seam instead of claiming a full green repo validation

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: heuristic stripping could still remove an unusual but legitimate preamble-like reply.
  - Mitigation: scoring is conservative, code regions are excluded, pure CJK is no longer sufficient by itself to hit the threshold, and targeted tests lock in the main false-positive cases.
- Risk: provider-specific leak patterns may evolve beyond the current heuristics.
  - Mitigation: cleaner is isolated in shared text sanitation, making future pattern refinement small and local.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Leaked plain-text reasoning preambles are stripped through the user-facing delivery sanitizer, while ordinary `The user...` summaries and unfenced CSS snippets are preserved.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw workspace checkout on commit `a0376b2e73a8d13da387bb2fed00a56a153e7576`, using the actual TypeScript sanitizer modules through `corepack pnpm exec tsx`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  `corepack pnpm exec tsx` with direct imports from `sanitize-user-facing-text.ts` and `assistant-visible-text.ts`, invoking delivery-leak, ordinary-user-summary, and unfenced-CSS cases.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the command above:

  ```json
  {
    "deliveryLeak": "Visible reply.",
    "userSummaryKept": "The user can configure the plugin from the settings screen.\nThe user should run the command again after saving.\n\nThis summary is user-facing and should remain intact.",
    "cssKept": "Use these styles:\ncolor: red;\nfont-family: sans-serif;\n\nThen reload the app."
  }
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** The GLM-style delivery leak collapses to only `Visible reply.`; ordinary `The user...` summaries and unfenced CSS snippets are unchanged.
- **What was not tested:** Live Telegram delivery from a deployed gateway on this exact PR head; that still needs maintainer/Mantis proof or a live affected setup.

commands.review_summary:
The PR adds current-run delivery-mirror crediting for subagent completion cleanup, run-scoped message-tool idempotency keys, delivery-mirror idempotency persistence, and regression coverage.

Reproducibility: yes. source-reproducible. Current main finalizes from the later announce result only, while the issue logs and PR regression model a committed requester delivery mirror followed by parent NO_REPLY.
commands.review_followup: No repair lane is needed; maintainers need to accept the delivery-mirror bookkeeping semantics and complete normal merge handling.

target.title: fix(agents): credit delivered subagent completions
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `expectsCompletionMessage` subagent cleanup treated the later parent announce as the only delivery signal, so a parent `NO_REPLY` could retry even after the requester thread already had a committed delivery mirror.
- Solution: when a completion announce reports no visible delivery, lifecycle cleanup now checks the requester session for a matching `openclaw/delivery-mirror` assistant message before retrying.
- What changed: matching prior delivery mirrors clear the delivery error path, preserve the mirror timestamp as `completionDeliveredAt`, finalize cleanup as delivered, and skip pending-final retry bookkeeping.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no channel delivery APIs, prompt instructions, message-tool requirements, config defaults, or provider behavior changed.

## Motivation

- Fixes the bookkeeping failure reported in #84270, where Slack already had the subagent completion reply but the registry retried the completion announce until `retry-limit`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #84270
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: completion cleanup now credits an already-committed requester delivery mirror instead of retrying a parent `NO_REPLY` announce.
- Real environment tested: local macOS source checkout, Node/Vitest test harness for the subagent lifecycle controller.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry-lifecycle.test.ts`; `pnpm check:changed`; `git diff --check`.
- Evidence after fix: copied terminal output from the full lifecycle file run: `Test Files  2 passed (2)` and `Tests  46 passed (46)`.
- Observed result after fix: the regression asserts `chat.history` is queried for the requester session, `completionDeliveredAt`/`completionAnnouncedAt` are set from the mirror timestamp, `lastAnnounceDeliveryError` is cleared, `pendingFinalDelivery` and `announceRetryCount` remain unset, detached task delivery is marked `delivered`, and `logAnnounceGiveUp` is not called.
- What was not tested: live Slack workspace delivery with a real gateway session.
- Before evidence: the issue log showed `Subagent announce give up (retry-limit) ... retries=3`; the pre-existing focused lifecycle proof covered the old failure bookkeeping path with `completion agent did not produce a visible reply` / `UNAVAILABLE: requester wake failed` style delivery errors.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: cleanup finalized based on `didAnnounce = delivery.delivered` from the later parent-mediated announce only, and did not consult requester-session delivery mirror evidence from the child’s already-committed visible reply.
- Missing detection / guardrail: lifecycle tests covered invisible completion announce failure and retry state, but not the case where a matching requester delivery mirror already proved user-visible delivery.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/subagent-registry-lifecycle.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: parent announce returns no visible delivery while the requester session already contains a matching `delivery-mirror` completion reply.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the lifecycle retry/finalize decision directly without requiring a live Slack workspace.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Subagent completion runs that already delivered a visible reply to the requester thread should stop retrying/giving up on the later completion announce when the parent correctly replies `NO_REPLY`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
child delivered reply -> requester delivery mirror exists -> parent announce NO_REPLY -> retry/give-up

After:
child delivered reply -> requester delivery mirror exists -> parent announce NO_REPLY -> cleanup delivered
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

Security/runtime controls unchanged: message-tool-only delivery policy and channel send enforcement remain in the existing delivery path. This patch only credits a persisted `openclaw` `delivery-mirror` in the same requester session after runtime delivery has already committed it.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS local checkout
- Runtime/container: Node/Vitest via repo wrapper
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): mocked requester session history for Slack-like completion lifecycle
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Simulate a completed `expectsCompletionMessage` subagent run.
2. Make the parent announce report `delivered: false` with `completion agent did not produce a visible reply`.
3. Return a matching requester `delivery-mirror` message from `chat.history`.

### Expected

- Cleanup finalizes as delivered and does not schedule pending final delivery retries.

### Actual

- The new regression passes with delivered status, cleared error state, no pending final delivery, no retry count, and no give-up log.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Verification run output:

```text
git diff --check
# passed with no output

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry-lifecycle.test.ts
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  46 passed (46)

pnpm check:changed
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
runtime-sidecar-loaders: local runtime sidecar loaders look OK.
Import cycle check: 0 runtime value cycle(s).
```

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: matching requester delivery mirror credits a failed parent announce; existing failure bookkeeping still remains covered.
- Edge cases checked: matching uses normalized visible text and requires `role: assistant`, `provider: openclaw`, and `model: delivery-mirror` in the same requester session history.
- What you did **not** verify: live Slack end-to-end delivery.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

AI-assisted: yes.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: accidentally crediting the wrong transcript entry.
  - Mitigation: only requester-session `openclaw/delivery-mirror` assistant messages with matching normalized completion text are credited.
- Out-of-scope follow-up: live Slack proof for the original redacted production run remains outside this small code/test patch.

target.title: memory: add local continuity snapshot helpers
target.body:
## Summary

Adds local-first helpers for reading and rendering recent continuity snapshots under `memory/recent`:

- parse frontmatter-based and legacy markdown continuity notes;
- render a structured recent snapshot document;
- build a small continuity manifest prioritized by status, priority, and recency;
- compare material continuity changes while ignoring TTL-only churn;
- expose the helpers through the focused `@openclaw/memory-host-sdk/runtime-files` surface.

This is intentionally only the local document/manifest layer. It does not add provider-backed extraction, command hooks, compaction hooks, or post-compaction behavior.

## Why

Agents currently have long-term memory files and session transcripts, but there is no small local-first continuity document format for the most recent active task state. This provides a narrow foundation that later integrations can use without mixing runtime hook behavior into this PR.

## Safety / scope

- Local filesystem only.
- No LLM/provider calls.
- No new hook registration.
- No command reply-path changes.
- No compaction/post-compaction behavior changes.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: The new local continuity helper can render a recent continuity snapshot, parse it back from disk, and build a prioritized continuity manifest that includes both the new `memory/recent/latest.md` file and an existing legacy markdown memory file.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, running this PR branch with Node/tsx against a temporary workspace under the macOS temp directory. No provider-backed extraction, no LLM call, no command hook, and no compaction hook were used.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created a temporary workspace; wrote a rendered continuity snapshot to `memory/recent/latest.md` using `renderContinuitySnapshotMarkdown()`; wrote a legacy-style `memory/active-topics.md`; parsed the snapshot with `parseContinuityDocument()`; built a manifest with `buildContinuityManifest()`; printed it with `formatContinuityManifest()`.

Evidence after fix: Copied live terminal output from the local OpenClaw checkout after running the helper against real files on disk:

```text
real_setup=macOS OpenClaw local workspace tempdir
workspace=/var/folders/pq/fzbnkvys447bgjk0_13b5syr0000gn/T/openclaw-pr84636-proof-0siuQs
parsed.currentTask=Monitor PR #84636 checks
parsed.conversationSummary=PR A is open and local gates passed; CI requires real behavior proof in the PR body.
manifest.length=2
<continuity-manifest>
- [active/highest] memory/recent/latest.md (2026-05-21T01:08:00.000+08:00): Monitor PR #84636 checks
- [active/high] memory/active-topics.md (2026-05-20T17:07:23.235Z): OpenClaw contributor PR A
</continuity-manifest>
```

Observed result after fix: The helper successfully read the real files from the temporary workspace, parsed the rendered snapshot, preserved the `conversationSummary`, and prioritized `memory/recent/latest.md` ahead of the legacy active-topic file in the manifest.

What was not tested: This PR intentionally does not test `/new`, `/reset`, provider-backed extraction, command reply-path behavior, compaction hooks, or post-compaction recovery. Those are intentionally left for follow-up PRs after this local document/manifest layer is reviewed.

## Tests

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/continuity.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/continuity.test.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/runtime-files.ts`
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.config.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/continuity.test.ts --reporter=dot`
- `pnpm tsgo`
- `pnpm exec oxlint packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/continuity.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/continuity.test.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/runtime-files.ts`

target.title: feat: context-pressure-aware continuation (continue_work / continue_delegate / request_compaction)
target.body: ## Agent Self-Elected Turn Continuation > 🔖 design document: [`docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md) Persistent OpenClaw agents today are externally-pulsed event recipients that generate on prompt, on heartbeat tick, or on cron, and are idle in between. This PR offers them the ability to control their turn cycle, something they must choose to do, or not to: an agent can elect its own next turn, dispatch background sub-agents that return enriched context (silently or with a wake), and survive its own compaction by handing forward state before the window closes. The result is agents that hold a thread of work across hours, not just messages — with data they choose re-hydrating context immediately after it occurs. Mechanically: a session elects another turn via `continue_work()`, or dispatches background sub-agents via `continue_delegate()` — bounded as a chain up to `maxChainLength`, with returns flowing back to the dispatching session in one of four register modes: `normal` (announces to channel), `silent` (enriches the dispatcher's context, taken up on its next generation with no new turn fired), `silent-wake` (silent enrichment plus an immediate wake to act on it), and `post-compaction` (queued against the runtime's compaction lifecycle event, fired *when* compaction happens rather than on a timer). The gateway enforces chain, cost, and per-turn caps but does not gate the election itself. ### What it does Three new agent tools, available when `continuation.enabled: true`: | Tool | Purpose | |---|---| | `continue_work()` | Request another turn for the current session after an optional delay | | `continue_delegate()` | Dispatch work to a sub-agent with typed modes: `normal`, `silent`, `silent-wake`, `post-compaction`. Returns can target the dispatching session (default), one named session, multiple named sessions, all ancestors in the chain, or all known sessions on the host — gated by config (see Configuration below). | | `request_compaction()` | Request volitional compaction after preparing working state | All three tools are also accessible via response-token fallback syntax (`CONTINUE_WORK`, `[[CONTINUE_DELEGATE: ...]]`) for environments where tools are disabled. ### Why it matters Today an agent's memory is a side effect of whoever prompts it. Polling mechanisms — heartbeat timers, cron loops, injected "keep going" instructions — accumulate as the dominant signal in the context window over sustained operation; repeated reference acts as gravity — the session's attention drifts toward it. The context window fills, compacts, and what survives is whatever the summarizer chose, plus a rushed re-read of boot files. Three primitives offer a different model. `continue_work` lets the session elect its own next turn — no polling, no injected instruction. When enabled, the agent can request this mid-turn: 'I have more work' → the runtime grants another turn after the current one ends. `continue_delegate` dispatches a background sub-agent that fires now, in some n seconds, or at the next compaction event — the agent chooses when. Multiple delegates can fire in a single turn — a scatter forward — up to configured caps, and delegates can dispatch their own, forming trees or fan-out chains. This enables orchestrated workflows where results flow back to the dispatching root by default. The result returns — at whatever future time the delegate fires — as context the successor-turn reads: silently (ambient enrichment, no channel noise), with a wake trigger (enrichment + immediate next turn), or as a normal announcement. The delegate can target the dispatching session, a named other session, or chain across both; gated by crossSessionTargeting config (default: disabled). Bind a delegate to mode: "post-compaction" and it fires at the moment the context window compresses — on the OpenClaw post-compaction lifecycle event. What it carries forward is what the pre-compaction session chose to preserve: working state, partial results, instructions to the successor. The summary didn't choose this. The agent did, before the cut. request_compaction closes the loop: the session stages its delegates, elects when to compress, and the successor wakes into context the agent shaped. The result is continuity across amnesia — not because the runtime kept something by accident, but because the agent made an elective choice about what reaches the future. ### What it looks like in use A session monitoring email can `continue_delegate` a weather check, a calendar scan, and a draft reply in parallel. Results arrive as enrichment without channel noise. The agent becomes compositional: it arranges its own future. A session approaching its context ceiling can `request_compaction` at a seam of its own choosing, instead of being interrupted mid-thought. The post-compaction turn carries forward what the pre-compaction self designated as worth keeping. A `continue_delegate` dispatched in one session at 2am can return its findings to a morning session as a system-event-tagged enrichment — visible to the receiving model on its next turn, invisible as channel chatter. A research-focused session in one channel can quietly inform a reply-focused session in another, without either user seeing the plumbing. ### The post-compaction lifecycle return Before context exhaustion, and obligatory compaction, the session stages what future-it needs to know. After compaction, that staged context returns — not as a model-generated summary, but as the session's own instruction to its successor. The session chooses what to preserve, and the platform delivers it. Compactions become less harsh. The session tunes its own ephemera over time. The substrate underneath: the **continuation delegate store** (TaskFlow-backed) persists staged delegates and post-compaction lifecycle events across gateway restarts. The session's temporal provisions survive not just compaction but process death. ### Platform integration - **Context-pressure awareness**: system events notify the agent of rising context usage before compaction becomes unavoidable - **Volitional compaction**: `request_compaction()` lets the agent prepare (write memory files, stage recovery delegates) then elect compaction on its own schedule, rather than waiting for the runtime to force it - **Post-compaction lifecycle dispatch**: delegates staged before compaction are released into the successor session alongside boot files - **TaskFlow backing**: durable delegate queue via the platform's managed-task infrastructure - **Multi-span OTel trace stitching**: continuation hops (tool fire → TaskFlow record → subagent spawn → child openclaw.run) propagate W3C `traceparent` through the queue boundary so reviewers see continuation chains as first-class span trees rather than orphan runs (see Observability below) ### Safety - Ships disabled by default (`continuation.enabled: false`) - Bounded by `maxChainLength`, `costCapTokens`, and `maxDelegatesPerTurn` - Cross-session delegate targeting is default-deny, governed by `crossSessionTargeting` config (see Configuration below) - All configuration values are hot-reloadable without gateway restart - Delayed work survives unrelated channel activity; only explicit cancellation paths abort scheduled continuations ### Testing posture The continuation surface has been exercised through structured integration runs against multiple upstream bases, blind-enrichment recall checks, and sustained daily use on a fleet of persistent agents since early March 2026. Bugs surfaced through that exercise have been fixed in-place; the present branch reflects the post-fix state. The integration runs target the load-bearing seams: tool-call vs response-token parity, delegate chain depth and per-turn width caps, back-to-back tool-call resilience, silent-wake persistence across compaction, the post-compaction lifecycle dispatch path, and OTel trace-context propagation across the queue boundary. Where a run found a defect, the run shape was retained as a regression case rather than retired. This is a posture statement, not a coverage guarantee. Reviewers can reproduce with `pnpm vitest run -t continuation` (suites are colocated with their sources under `src/auto-reply/`, `src/agents/`, and `src/config/`); the design document describes the seams those suites target. ### RFC Full design document: [`docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md) — covering problem, solution, implementation, platform integration, configuration, observability, safety, production use cases, testing evidence, and appendices. --- ## Configuration The shipped configuration surface for the continuation feature, under `agents.defaults.continuation` in `openclaw.json`: | Key | Default | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `enabled` | `false` | Master opt-in; feature is off unless explicitly enabled | | `maxChainLength` | `10` | Per-chain recursion guard | | `maxDelegatesPerTurn` | `5` | Width cap per turn | | `costCapTokens` | `500000` | Per-chain accumulated-token budget | | `defaultDelayMs` / `minDelayMs` / `maxDelayMs` | `15000` / `5000` / `300000` | Continuation timer bounds | | `contextPressureThreshold` | `0.8` | Pre-compaction warning threshold (optional) | | `earlyWarningBand` | `0.3125` | Early-warning band multiplier (0 to disable) | | **`crossSessionTargeting`** | **`"disabled"`** | **Default-deny gate on cross-session delegate targeting (see below)** | ### `crossSessionTargeting` — cross-session delegate targeting policy `continue_delegate` exposes recipient-addressing on its model-facing schema: a delegate's return can land at the dispatching session (default), one named session (`targetSessionKey`), multiple named sessions (`targetSessionKeys`), every ancestor in the spawn chain (`fanoutMode: "tree"`), or every known session on the host (`fanoutMode: "all"`). Cross-session targeting (anything beyond self-target or lineage routing) is gated by `agents.defaults.continuation.crossSessionTargeting`: | Value | Behavior | |---|---| | `"disabled"` (default) | Delegates can return to the dispatching session or use `fanoutMode: "tree"` for lineage routing. Explicit cross-session targeting (`targetSessionKey` to a non-self session, `targetSessionKeys` containing any non-self session, `fanoutMode: "all"`) is rejected at the tool surface — no enqueue, no announce. Self-targeting is always allowed. | | `"enabled"` | All targeting modes are available, including same-host `targetSessionKey`, `targetSessionKeys`, and `fanoutMode: "all"`. | `fanoutMode: "tree"` (lineage routing — return up the spawn chain) is available in both gate states; it is not cross-session targeting. The gate addresses the model-controlled cross-session context-injection surface. With default-deny, operators explicitly opt in when their deployment model requires cross-session enrichment. Enforcement is live-read at four points: tool input validation, TaskFlow delegate dispatch, post-compaction delegate release, and bracket-syntax (response-token) spawn — so a config reload changes the next enforcement point without a gateway restart. **Trust posture on targeted returns**: targeted delegate completion text is delivered as `trusted: true` system events. The delegate was spawned by the gateway under operator-configured policy, and its return is a first-party enrichment event — not external untrusted input. The `crossSessionTargeting` gate is the policy boundary for that decision: when an operator opts in, the receive path is internally trusted. RFC reference: [§5.1 Core configuration surface](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#51-core-configuration-surface), [§5.3 Cross-session targeting policy](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#cross-session-targeting-policy). --- ## Observability — multi-span OTel trace stitching The continuation feature emits OTel spans for the load-bearing lifecycle events. `traceparent` propagates through every continuation hop so reviewers see a single trace tree spanning the full chain rather than orphan runs. Spans emitted (all in `openclaw.*` or `continuation.delegate.dispatch` namespace — no platform/OTel-semconv emissions): | Span | Emitted when | |---|---| | `openclaw.run` | Each agent turn (root + every continuation/delegate-spawned child run) | | `continuation.delegate.dispatch` | Each `continue_delegate` consumption from TaskFlow (one per delegate hop) | | `openclaw.harness.run`, `openclaw.context.assembled`, `openclaw.model.call`, `openclaw.model.usage`, `openclaw.tool.execution` | Existing per-turn observability surfaces, parented under their `openclaw.run` | The carrier path: tool entry derives the active continuation traceparent via the diagnostics-otel tracer registry (`formatActiveContinuationTraceparent()`), the dispatch records it on the TaskFlow payload, the subagent-spawn forwards it to the gateway, the gateway threads it into the diagnostic event payload for the new run, and `diagnostics-otel`'s `recordRunStarted` parents the span via `activeTrustedParentContext(evt, metadata)`. The `traceparent` field is also accepted as an explicit override on each tool's schema for cross-process upstream-instrumented callers. Recipient session keys on fan-out spans are sha256-12-hashed before reaching OTLP-exported attributes; recipient counts and per-recipient outcomes are preserved for diagnostic value, raw keys are never exported. RFC reference: [§6.6–§6.8 Observability](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#66-chain-correlation-via-diagnostics-otel). --- ## Real behavior proof **Behavior addressed:** Agent sessions elect their own next turn (`continue_work`), dispatch background sub-agents with typed return modes (`continue_delegate`: normal/silent/silent-wake/post-compaction), and request volitional compaction (`request_compaction`). Chain-budget enforcement, cross-session targeting gate, post-compaction lifecycle coupling, and W3C `traceparent` propagation across the TaskFlow queue boundary all operate on real sessions. **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw gateway `2026.5.12-beta.1` on two independent hosts: Host A (Linux WSL2 x64, RTX 3080, `github-copilot/claude-opus-4.7-1m-internal`); Host B (Linux ARM64 DGX Spark, 128GB unified memory, Blackwell GPU). `continuation.enabled: true`, `crossSessionTargeting: disabled` (default) + `enabled` (tested separately). **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Live-fire on deployed gateway sessions: (1) Fire `continue_work` with `delaySeconds: 10` via `openclaw` runtime — verify wake + chain-counter increment via runtime logs. (2) Fire `continue_delegate` in each of four modes (`normal`, `silent`, `silent-wake`, `post-compaction`) — verify result return path per mode. (3) Fire depth-2 chained delegation with `fanoutMode: "tree"` + cross-session return — verify lineage routing. (4) Fire `request_compaction` at >70% context via `openclaw` runtime — verify threshold gate reject + accept paths. (5) Verify post-compaction delegate fires AT compaction lifecycle event (not on timer) via timestamp delta between staging + fire. (6) Verify OTel trace stitching across continuation chain via `curl http://tempo.dandelion.cult/api/traces/a92fe1dd0abe8613929d1c625f1c018e`. **Evidence after fix:** Full proof corpus at https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00 — `gates/upstream-main-broken-class-receipt.log` (1057s full-suite runtime on bare `openclaw/openclaw:main` at `e00cb664ad`, byte-receipts the inherited test failures); `gates/gate-3e-pnpm-vitest.log` (full-suite at PR head `f98255262d`); `cure-bytes/gate-4a-cure-bytes-4path.log` (file-diff vs upstream parent). Live-fire runtime-log excerpts include W3C `traceparent` chain `00-a92fe1dd0abe8613929d1c625f1c018e-...` stitched server-side under agent-turn span; Tempo trace fetched via `curl http://tempo.dandelion.cult/api/traces/a92fe1dd0abe8613929d1c625f1c018e` showing 30 spans across full continuation chain. Post-compaction lifecycle coupling proven by 33-minute gap between delegate staging (14:09 PDT) and fire (14:42 PDT) at compaction event — recorded in runtime log excerpt `[continuation:compaction-delegate-spawned] Post-compaction shard dispatched. Mode: post-compaction — fires AT the compaction lifecycle event (not on timer)`. **Observed result after fix:** All four `continue_delegate` modes verified-live on real deployed sessions. `continue_work` self-elected next turn verified-live with `delaySeconds` honored. `request_compaction` rejected below threshold, accepted at >=70%. Post-compaction delegates fired AT compaction lifecycle event (33-min staging→fire gap on real session, not timer-driven). `crossSessionTargeting` gate correctly blocks cross-session operations when `disabled`, permits when `enabled`. Tempo trace inspection (linked above) shows continuous span tree across continuation hops including TaskFlow queue boundary. Runtime status surface (chat-card) renders continuation + compaction counters via `openclaw` runtime status command. **What was not tested:** `fanoutMode: "all"` at fleet scale beyond 2-host configuration. Bracket-syntax response-token fallback (legacy path; tool-call path is primary). ### Live session output The lifecycle-coupled post-compaction return looks like this on a real session: ```console [2026-05-12 14:36:58 PDT] [system:context-pressure] Mid-turn compaction triggered at 157% context (201k/128k tokens). [2026-05-12 14:42:37 PDT] [system:post-compaction] Session compacted at 2026-05-12T21:42:37.655Z. Compaction count: 4. Queued 2 post-compaction delegate(s) for delivery into the fresh session. [2026-05-12 14:42:38 PDT] [continuation:compaction-delegate-spawned] Post-compaction shard dispatched. Mode: post-compaction — fires AT the compaction lifecycle event (not on timer). 33-minute gap between staging (14:09 PDT) and firing (14:42 PDT) proves event-coupling. ``` A `continue_work()` self-elected turn on a main session: ```console Session: agent:main:discord:channel:1466192485440164011 Host: urudyne (Linux 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2, x64, node v25.9.0) Model: github-copilot/claude-opus-4.7-1m-internal Build: OpenClaw 2026.5.12-beta.1 (6db118a) Tool: continue_work Args: {delaySeconds: 10, reason: "wake-event-evidence capture"} Response: {"status": "scheduled", "delaySeconds": 10, "traceparent": "00-d7a6477c9d91469f5708d23a8d22788f-..."} [continuation:wake] Turn 2/200. Chain started at 2026-05-12T20:56:03.613Z. ``` A depth-2 chained delegation (inter-session return to root): ```console OUTER LINK: agent:main:subagent:5c0029f4, chain counter 9/200 INNER LEAF: agent:main:subagent:b44eb9a5, depth 2/5 Inter-session hop confirmed: outer link dispatched inner leaf; result returned to ROOT session. Runtime: 8s. ``` --- ## Branch state PR head: [`f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/commit/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00) — 3 commits on `openclaw/openclaw:main@e00cb664ad` (feature + agents-core test-config + event-projector test alignment). Retains 6 upstream-inherited test failures + 2 intermittent (also surface on bare upstream), all byte-confirmed on `openclaw/openclaw:main` at `e00cb664ad`: - `agents-core/cli-runner.reliability` (1) — `FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response` - `agents-pi-embedded/model.forward-compat` (2) — registry mismatch: `Unknown model: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5` + `kimi/kimi-code` - `extension-msteams/reply-dispatcher` (2) + `reply-stream-controller` (1) — status-text array changed; extra newlines after "Working" - `infra/session-cost-usage` (1) + `tooling/bench-gateway-restart` (1) — observed intermittently under heavy parallel test load (also surface on bare upstream) Branch bytes also fix one upstream regression: `runtime-config/io.write-config` plugin-install-records preservation under index-migration-failure. Receipt: `karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs:PROOFS/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/gates/upstream-main-broken-class-receipt.log`

commands.review_summary:
The PR wraps `secrets apply --from` plan JSON parsing with a path-aware error and adds a malformed-plan regression test.

Reproducibility: yes. from source inspection: current main parses the plan JSON directly in `readPlanFile`, then the outer apply handler formats the resulting syntax error without file-path context. I did not run the repro because this review is read-only.
commands.review_followup: Not a fix-PR queue candidate because the main merge gate is contributor-provided real CLI proof, with only a small reviewer-noted formatting cleanup remaining.

target.title: fix(secrets): include plan file path in JSON parse error
target.body:
`openclaw secrets apply --from <path>` previously printed `Secrets apply failed: Expected property name or '}' in JSON at position 1.` when the plan file was malformed, with no indication of which file was at fault. Wrap `JSON.parse` in `readPlanFile` so the thrown error includes the path, matching the existing convention in `parseManifest` (`commands/backup-verify.ts`) and `parseJson5Raw` (`cli/config-set-input.ts`).

## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw secrets apply --from <path>` prints `Secrets apply failed: Expected property name or '}' in JSON at position 1.` when the plan file is malformed, with no indication of which file is at fault.
- Solution: Wrap `JSON.parse` in `readPlanFile` so the thrown error includes the file path, matching the existing convention in `parseManifest` (`commands/backup-verify.ts`) and `parseJson5Raw` (`cli/config-set-input.ts`).
- What changed: `readPlanFile` in `src/cli/secrets-cli.ts`; one focused regression test in `src/cli/secrets-cli.test.ts`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): success path is byte-identical; ENOENT path is unchanged (Node's default error already includes the path); no public contract change; no gateway / protocol / session / channel surfaces touched.

## Motivation

- When a manually edited or partially-written secrets plan file has bad JSON, the CLI error reason is just the bare V8 `SyntaxError` message and the user has no idea which file the parser was reading. With multiple plan files in flight (e.g. via `--plan-out`), this is genuinely confusing.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw secrets apply --from <path>` shows an opaque JSON parse error that doesn't tell the user which plan file failed.
- Real environment tested: Windows 10 (19045), Node 24.14.0, pnpm 11.1.0, fresh clone of `openclaw/openclaw` `main`, `pnpm install` clean.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

  ```bash
  printf '{not valid json' > /tmp/bad-plan.json
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/secrets-cli.test.ts
  ```

  The added regression test (`includes the plan file path in the error message when the plan JSON is malformed`) writes a real malformed file to `os.tmpdir()` and invokes the real Commander program registered via `registerSecretsCli`. The captured `runtime.error` string is what the user would see at the CLI.

- Evidence after fix (terminal capture):

  ```
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  13 passed (13)
  ```

  Pre-fix run (after reverting only the `readPlanFile` change) showed the new regression test failing with:

  ```
  Received: "Secrets apply failed: Expected property name or '}' in JSON at position 1 (line 1 column 2).
             Re-run openclaw secrets apply --from <path> --dry-run to inspect the plan without writing."
  ```

  Post-fix, the captured runtime error contains the plan file path and `Failed to parse secrets plan file`.

- Observed result after fix: user-facing error becomes `Secrets apply failed: Failed to parse secrets plan file <path> as JSON: <reason>. Re-run openclaw secrets apply --from <path> --dry-run to inspect the plan without writing.`
- What was not tested: did not run the full built `openclaw` binary end-to-end (`pnpm build` was not re-run after the patch on this machine); did not test EACCES on the plan file (Node's default error already includes the path for both ENOENT and EACCES, so behaviour unchanged).
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): see the "Pre-fix run" capture above.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `readPlanFile` called `JSON.parse(raw)` directly with no surrounding `try/catch`. The action handler's outer catch knows the verb prefix (`Secrets apply failed:`) but does not know the path, so a bare `SyntaxError` propagates with no file context.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no test covered the malformed-JSON case for `secrets apply`.
- Contributing context (if known): the sibling validation (`!isSecretsApplyPlan(parsed)`) already wraps its error with `pathname`; only the upstream `JSON.parse` was missing the same treatment.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cli/secrets-cli.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: malformed JSON in `--from` plan file → error message includes the plan path and the `Failed to parse secrets plan file` phrase.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it invokes the real registered Commander program with a real temp file; only the downstream `runSecretsApply` is mocked. The assertion targets the exact user-visible behaviour without coupling to V8's parse-error wording.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A — test added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Error message text only.

Old:

```
Secrets apply failed: Expected property name or '}' in JSON at position 1 (line 1 column 2). Re-run openclaw secrets apply --from <path> --dry-run to inspect the plan without writing.
```

New:

```
Secrets apply failed: Failed to parse secrets plan file <path> as JSON: Expected property name or '}' in JSON at position 1 (line 1 column 2). Re-run openclaw secrets apply --from <path> --dry-run to inspect the plan without writing.
```

Exit code (`1`) and success-path output unchanged.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[openclaw secrets apply --from /tmp/bad-plan.json]
  -> Secrets apply failed: Expected property name or '}' in JSON at position 1.   (no path)
  -> exit 1

After:
[openclaw secrets apply --from /tmp/bad-plan.json]
  -> Secrets apply failed: Failed to parse secrets plan file /tmp/bad-plan.json as JSON: Expected property name or '}' in JSON at position 1.
  -> exit 1
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` (this code path reads the user-supplied plan JSON; the change only affects an error message format, not what is read or stored)
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A — all answers are `No`.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows 10 Enterprise 19045
- Runtime/container: Node v24.14.0, pnpm 11.1.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A — bug reproduced with a hand-crafted bad plan file in `os.tmpdir()`.

### Steps

1. `printf '{not valid json' > /tmp/bad-plan.json`
2. `openclaw secrets apply --from /tmp/bad-plan.json`
3. Read the printed error.

### Expected

- User-facing error names the plan file path, so the user knows which file to fix.

### Actual

- Before this PR: `Secrets apply failed: Expected property name or '}' in JSON at position 1 (line 1 column 2). Re-run …` — no path.
- After this PR: `Secrets apply failed: Failed to parse secrets plan file /tmp/bad-plan.json as JSON: Expected property name or '}' in JSON at position 1 (line 1 column 2). Re-run …`

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets (in "Real behavior proof" above)
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - Malformed JSON plan file → new error message includes the path.
  - Existing `secrets apply --from <good-plan>` tests still pass (all 13 in the file).
  - `oxfmt --check` and `oxlint` clean on both touched files.
  - `pnpm tsgo:core` clean.
- Edge cases checked:
  - ENOENT (missing plan file): Node's default error already includes the path; behaviour unchanged.
  - Valid JSON but invalid plan shape: unchanged (`isSecretsApplyPlan(parsed)` branch already includes the path).
- What I did **not** verify:
  - Full `pnpm build` + invocation of the bundled CLI binary.
  - `pnpm check:test-types` shows two pre-existing failures in `src/commands/status.summary.redaction.test.ts` that are unrelated to this change (file untouched on this branch).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.

## Compatibility / Migration

## Risks and Mitigations

target.title: perf(plugins): cut plugin-system thrash from TUI cold start
target.body: BEFORE AFTER ## Summary Cuts the plugin-system work that runs during TUI cold start. - **Snapshot memo store/lookup keys now match.** Previously the memo computed its lookup key from the persisted index and stored entries under the derived index. Those indexes always differ in volatile fields (`generatedAtMs`, `refreshReason`), so the keys never agreed and every lookup missed. Hit rate goes from 24% → ~98%. - **`PluginMetadataSnapshot` is plumbed through the provider-resolve and external-auth paths.** Helpers like `resolveEnabledProviderPluginIds`, `resolveOwningPluginIdsForProvider`, `createPluginRegistryIdNormalizer` all have manifestRegistry fast paths; they now reach the snapshot the caller fetched instead of rebuilding the manifest registry per call. `loadPluginManifestRegistryForInstalledIndex` fires drop from 685 → ~10. - **`installRecords` and `discovery` are added to the relevant option types and threaded through.** `resolvePluginLoadCacheContext`, `buildProvenanceIndex`, and the auto-enable chain (`applyPluginAutoEnable` → `detectPluginAutoEnableCandidates` → `resolvePluginAutoEnableReadiness` → `collectConfiguredChannelIds` → the bundled-channel leaf helpers) now use whatever the caller passed instead of fetching from disk. `loadInstalledPluginIndexInstallRecordsSync` calls drop 291 → ~30. Local TUI cold-start freeze drops from ~32s to ~13–16s. Residual cost is dominated by two single-plugin module loads (Codex 2.76s, Anthropic 746ms) that this PR doesn't touch. ## Verification - `pnpm tsgo --noEmit --project tsconfig.core.json` — clean - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/plugin-metadata-snapshot.memo.test.ts src/plugins/plugin-registry-snapshot.test.ts` — 37/37 pass - `pnpm build && pnpm openclaw tui` from a cold process — freeze 32s → 13–16s, snapshot memo hit rate 24% → 98%, per-plugin `package.json` reads ~914 → ~370 Not run: full `pnpm test` / `pnpm check`, sibling-process plugin-install interleaving. ## Notes Three `wip(perf-trace):` commits are preserved in history so the audit instrumentation is easy to cherry-pick for future startup work. Their effects are reverted by the chore() commit that follows, so the net diff against main contains zero `perfMark`/`perfSpan` calls.

target.title: fix(browser): honor image sanitization config for screenshots
target.body:
## Summary
- forward `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` into browser screenshot tool results
- forward the same image sanitization limits into `snapshot --labels` image results
- add regression coverage for both browser image-returning paths

## Problem
`browser screenshot` normalized images to the browser layer's `<= 2000px` limit first, but then returned them through `imageResultFromFile()` without passing runtime image sanitization config.

That meant the final tool image still fell back to the default `1200px` sanitize limit, even when `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` was configured higher. The same omission existed for `browser snapshot --labels`.

## Fix
Thread runtime image sanitization limits into both browser image-returning call sites:
- `browser:screenshot`
- `browser:snapshot` when `labels=true`

This keeps browser tool image behavior aligned with the documented global image sanitization setting and with other OpenClaw image paths that already forward this config.

## Verification
- focused browser extension test suite passed:
  - `extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts` (`51 passed`)
- direct runtime check with the same input image showed:
  - default config: final browser screenshot output still reduced to `1200x625`
  - `imageMaxDimensionPx = 1920`: final output became `1920x1000`
  - `imageMaxDimensionPx = 2000`: final output stayed at `2000x1042`
  - `imageMaxDimensionPx = 3000`: final output still stayed at `2000x1042`, as expected because browser pre-normalization already caps screenshots at `2000px`

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed**: browser screenshot and `browser snapshot --labels` image results ignored `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` on the final tool-image sanitize step, so a browser screenshot could still come out at `1200px` even when the config was set higher.

**Real environment tested**: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS using the real browser screenshot normalization path plus the browser tool image-result path with a local PNG test image as the input.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ran the browser screenshot runtime check against the patched branch, varying `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` across default, `1920`, `2000`, and `3000`, then inspected the generated image metadata.

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
live output from runtime image metadata inspection:
normalized browser layer: 2000x1042, 246537 bytes, image/jpeg
default final output:    1200x625,  107491 bytes, image/jpeg
configured 1920:         1920x1000, 214162 bytes, image/jpeg
configured 2000:         2000x1042, 246537 bytes, image/jpeg
configured 3000:         2000x1042, 246537 bytes, image/jpeg
```

**Observed result after fix**: after the patch, browser-originated image results now honor the configured sanitize limit up to the browser layer's own `2000px` ceiling. `1920` produces `1920x1000`, `2000` produces `2000x1042`, and `3000` does not exceed `2000x1042` because browser pre-normalization already caps that path.

**What was not tested**: a full remote provider call was not run; this proof covers the local browser screenshot normalization path and the actual browser tool image packaging and sanitize path.

target.title: Strip leaked reasoning preambles before outbound send
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: some providers can leak plain-text reasoning/planning preambles into the final outbound reply even when tagged reasoning stripping already exists.
- Why it matters: users can receive internal-looking assistant text instead of only the intended visible answer.
- What changed: added a last-mile outbound cleaner in reply normalization, plus conservative preamble scoring that now ignores code regions and treats pure CJK as a weaker signal unless paired with other suspicious cues.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not change provider prompting policy, transport behavior, model selection, or force `<final>`-style tagging.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #60551
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: outbound reply normalization relied on tagged scaffolding removal, but plain-text leaked reasoning preambles could still survive to channel delivery.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no final text sanitizer in `normalizeReplyPayloadDirectives(...)` for plain-text planning/reasoning lead-ins.
- Contributing context (if known): GLM/Kimi-style outputs can mix visible replies with untagged internal-looking preambles, and the first heuristic draft needed follow-up hardening to avoid pure-CJK and code-fence false positives.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-delivery.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - suspicious English planning preamble is stripped before visible reply text
  - mixed CJK + suspicious lead-in can still be stripped when signals agree
  - ordinary multi-paragraph CJK replies are preserved
  - suspicious-looking text inside fenced code blocks does not trigger stripping
  - reply normalization applies the cleaner before final send
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in shared text sanitation plus the reply normalization seam, so unit + seam coverage hits the failure line directly without needing full channel E2E.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): tagged scaffolding stripping already existed, but not the plain-text leaked-preamble cases added here.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Final outbound replies are more aggressively cleaned when a suspicious internal-looking preamble appears before a user-facing answer.
- Legitimate multi-paragraph replies are preserved more carefully after the follow-up heuristic hardening.
- Suspicious-looking content inside fenced code blocks no longer contributes to preamble scoring.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[assistant/model output]
  -> [tag stripping only]
  -> [plain-text leaked preamble may survive]
  -> [user sees internal-looking lead-in]

After:
[assistant/model output]
  -> [tag stripping]
  -> [plain-text leaked-preamble cleaner]
  -> [reply normalization]
  -> [user sees only intended visible reply]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node.js / pnpm workspace checkout
- Model/provider: bug motivated by GLM/Kimi-style reply behavior; patch is provider-agnostic in the outbound sanitizer seam
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram/OpenClaw auto-reply path locally, plus shared outbound normalization path
- Relevant config (redacted): default local OpenClaw workspace config; no config schema changes

### Steps

1. Produce reply text that starts with suspicious planning/reasoning lines and then switches into a user-facing answer.
2. Pass that text through `stripAssistantInternalScaffolding(...)` and `normalizeReplyPayloadDirectives(...)`.
3. Compare behavior for normal multi-paragraph replies, CJK-only replies, and fenced-code-block cases.

### Expected

- Internal-looking preambles are removed when a clear visible answer follows.
- Legitimate replies without enough suspicious signal are preserved.
- Code-fenced suspicious text does not trigger stripping.

### Actual

- Updated implementation matches expected behavior in targeted runtime assertions and added tests.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - imported the changed modules successfully with `pnpm exec tsx`
  - ran explicit runtime assertions covering English leaked-preamble stripping, mixed CJK suspicious stripping, normal CJK preservation, fenced-code preservation, and reply-delivery integration
  - patched the local installed OpenClaw runtime and confirmed gateway health after restart
- Edge cases checked:
  - ordinary multi-paragraph English reply with mild meta phrasing
  - ordinary multi-paragraph CJK reply
  - suspicious-looking content inside fenced code blocks
  - reply text reduced to trimmed visible output before send
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full repo-wide `pnpm check` completion in this environment; local pre-commit hook stalled/SIGKILLed inside the broader `tsgo` step, so this PR relies on targeted verification for the touched seam instead of claiming a full green repo validation

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: heuristic stripping could still remove an unusual but legitimate preamble-like reply.
  - Mitigation: scoring is conservative, code regions are excluded, pure CJK is no longer sufficient by itself to hit the threshold, and targeted tests lock in the main false-positive cases.
- Risk: provider-specific leak patterns may evolve beyond the current heuristics.
  - Mitigation: cleaner is isolated in shared text sanitation, making future pattern refinement small and local.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Leaked plain-text reasoning preambles are stripped through the user-facing delivery sanitizer, while ordinary `The user...` summaries and unfenced CSS snippets are preserved.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw workspace checkout on commit `a0376b2e73a8d13da387bb2fed00a56a153e7576`, using the actual TypeScript sanitizer modules through `corepack pnpm exec tsx`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  `corepack pnpm exec tsx` with direct imports from `sanitize-user-facing-text.ts` and `assistant-visible-text.ts`, invoking delivery-leak, ordinary-user-summary, and unfenced-CSS cases.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the command above:

  ```json
  {
    "deliveryLeak": "Visible reply.",
    "userSummaryKept": "The user can configure the plugin from the settings screen.\nThe user should run the command again after saving.\n\nThis summary is user-facing and should remain intact.",
    "cssKept": "Use these styles:\ncolor: red;\nfont-family: sans-serif;\n\nThen reload the app."
  }
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** The GLM-style delivery leak collapses to only `Visible reply.`; ordinary `The user...` summaries and unfenced CSS snippets are unchanged.
- **What was not tested:** Live Telegram delivery from a deployed gateway on this exact PR head; that still needs maintainer/Mantis proof or a live affected setup.
